question: Glashow-Weinberg-Salam: what's the electroweak gauge group and the unifying idea?

options: $SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ with couplings $g, g'$; LH fermions in $SU(2)$ doublets, RH in singlets; spontaneously broken to $U(1)_{\rm EM}$ by Higgs vev; physical bosons: $W^\pm$, $Z$, $\gamma$ via mixing $(W^3, B) \to (Z, \gamma)$ with Weinberg angle $\tan\theta_W = g'/g$
$SU(2)$ alone, unified gauge group (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship)
$SU(3) \times U(1)$ — same as QCD plus EM (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
$SO(10)$ at low energies, broken to electromagnetism (this conflates cause and effect) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: GWS model (1961–68): unify weak and EM by enlarging $U(1)_{\rm EM}$ to $SU(2)_L\times U(1)_Y$. Gauge fields: $W^a_\mu$ ($a=1,2,3$) for $SU(2)_L$, $B_\mu$ for $U(1)_Y$. Doublets: $(\nu_e, e)_L$, $(u, d)_L$ etc. Hypercharge $Y$: $Q = T_3 + Y$, e.g.\ $Y_L = -1/2$ (lepton doublet), $Y_{e_R} = -1$. Higgs doublet $\Phi$ with $Y = 1/2$ gets vev $v = 246$ GeV → 3 of 4 gauge bosons get mass ($W^\pm, Z$), photon remains massless. Mixing: $A_\mu = \cos\theta_W B_\mu + \sin\theta_W W^3_\mu$, $Z_\mu = -\sin\theta_W B_\mu + \cos\theta_W W^3_\mu$; $\sin^2\theta_W \approx 0.231$.

active.prompt: Match each electroweak boson with its mass and role:

pairs.definition: Charged weak boson; mass 80.4 GeV; mediates charged-current (flavour-changing) interactions
Neutral weak boson; mass 91.2 GeV; mediates neutral current; discovered 1973 at Gargamelle
Massless gauge boson of unbroken $U(1)_{\rm EM}$; QED's mediator
Scalar excitation of the Higgs field; mass 125 GeV; discovered 2012 at ATLAS/CMS

active.explanation: Mass relations: $M_W = gv/2$, $M_Z = \sqrt{g^2 + g'^2}\,v/2$, so $M_W/M_Z = \cos\theta_W$ at tree level. This 'rho parameter' $\rho = M_W^2/(M_Z^2\cos^2\theta_W) = 1$ at tree level, gets small ($\lesssim 1\%$) corrections — extremely tight test of SM. Z and W discovered at CERN UA1/UA2 (1983), Higgs at LHC (2012).

question: What's the Weinberg angle and how is it measured?

options: $\sin\theta_W = g'/\sqrt{g^2 + g'^2}$ parametrises mixing of $W^3$ and $B$ to give $Z$ and $\gamma$; measured via $M_W/M_Z$ ratio, $Z$-pole asymmetries, neutrino-electron scattering, atomic parity violation; on-shell value $\sin^2\theta_W \approx 0.2229$, $\overline{MS}$ $\sin^2\hat\theta_W \approx 0.2312$; values depend on renormalisation scheme

$\theta_W$ is the angle between proton and neutron in $\beta$-decay (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)

$\sin^2\theta_W = 1$ exactly — no mixing in modern view (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Weinberg angle is the QCD running coupling at $m_Z$ (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation: Defines mixing of neutral gauge bosons: $\begin{pmatrix}A\\Z\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta_W & \sin\theta_W \\ -\sin\theta_W & \cos\theta_W\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}B\\W^3\end{pmatrix}$. Photon couples to $Q = T_3 + Y$ with strength $e = g\sin\theta_W = g'\cos\theta_W$. Measurements: (i) $M_W/M_Z$ → on-shell scheme; (ii) forward-backward asymmetry $A_{FB}$ at $Z$ pole (LEP, SLD); (iii) atomic parity violation in cesium; (iv) Møller scattering $e^-e^-$ at SLAC E158; (v) NuTeV $\nu N$ scattering. Different schemes (on-shell vs.\ $\overline{MS}$ vs.\ effective leptonic) differ by few-permille — central to precision EW tests.

options:
$ ar
u_\mu + e^- 	o  ar
u_\mu + e^-$ via $Z$ exchange (no charged-current alternative since $ ar
u_\mu$ can't make $\mu^-$); first observed at Gargamelle bubble chamber, CERN, 1973; also hadronic $
u_\mu N 	o 
u_\mu X$
$e^+e^- \to \mu^+\mu^-$ — clean leptonic NC test (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
$\beta$ decay — first observation of weak interactions (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general)
$\gamma\gamma \to W^+W^-$ pair production (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation:
Pre-1973: only charged-current weak interactions known ($ eta$ decay etc.). GWS predicted $Z$ exchange → neutral currents preserving lepton flavour. Discovery: Gargamelle heavy-liquid bubble chamber at CERN, 1973, observed $ ar
u_\mu e 	o  ar
u_\mu e$ scattering events. Hadronic NC discovery simultaneous: $
u_\mu N 	o 
u_\mu X$ (no muon in final state). Confirmation at HPWF (Fermilab). Direct $Z$ production at $p ar p 	o Z + X$ (UA1, 1983). LEP Z factory (1989-2000) measured $Z$ properties to permille precision; $\Gamma_Z = 2.495$ GeV, $N_
u = 2.984$ (three light neutrino species).

question: Without spontaneous symmetry breaking, the SM has bad UV behaviour. Cite an example needing the Higgs.

options: $W_L W_L \to W_L W_L$ scattering (longitudinal Ws): without Higgs, amplitude grows as $E^2/v^2$ and violates unitarity around $\sqrt s \sim 1$ TeV; Higgs exchange cancels bad high-energy growth via $h$ couplings $\propto m_W^2/v$ — restores unitarity precisely (signature of gauge-Higgs interplay)
QED running coupling diverges at Landau pole; needs Higgs to fix (true only as a special case, not in general) (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Quark confinement breaks down without the Higgs field (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon)
Neutrino oscillations would not occur without the Higgs (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (not supported by the evidence) (this conflates cause and effect)
passive.explanation: Equivalence theorem: at high energy, longitudinal $W$/$Z$ scattering ≈ Goldstone scattering ($\pi^a$ from broken $SU(2)$). Without Higgs as new state, $W_L W_L \to W_L W_L$ amplitude grows as $s/v^2$; partial-wave unitarity ($|a_0| \le 1$) violated at $\sqrt s \sim \sqrt{16\pi v^2} \sim 1.2$ TeV. Higgs $s$- and $t$-channel exchange supplies amplitude $\propto -s/v^2$ that exactly cancels growing piece — final amplitude flat at high $E$. This 'unitarisation' is theoretical evidence that something Higgs-like must exist below $\sim 1$ TeV. Found at 125 GeV (2012), well within the bound. Alternative unitarisations (technicolour, composite Higgs) would manifest as new resonances near 1 TeV — being probed at LHC.

question: Anomaly cancellation in the SM: what mathematical condition is satisfied and why is it remarkable?

options: Sum of $Y^3, Y\cdot T^2, Y\cdot c$ (and $SU(2)^3$, gravitational) over each generation vanishes — chiral fermion content arranged so triangle anomalies cancel between quarks and leptons within each family. Requires quarks to come in 3 colours; provides theoretical link colour-anomaly cancellation
Only gauge bosons contribute to anomalies; fermions are irrelevant (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Anomalies cancel by adding extra heavy fermions; not from SM content alone (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence)
SM is anomalous; consistent only as an effective theory (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Gauge anomalies from chiral fermion triangle diagrams. For $U(1)_Y$: $\sum_L Y_L^3 - \sum_R Y_R^3 = 0$ must hold (similarly for mixed $SU(2)^2 Y$, $SU(3)^2 Y$, gravitational $Y$). With one SM family: $L: 2\cdot(-1/2)^3$, $e_R: -(-1)^3$, $Q: 3\cdot 2\cdot(1/6)^3$, $u_R: -3\cdot(2/3)^3$, $d_R: -3\cdot(-1/3)^3$ → carefully sum to zero. Factor of 3 from colour: anomaly cancellation requires $N_c = 3$ given lepton-quark assignment. Beautiful consistency check; major hint that SM is part of grand unification (e.g.\ SU(5), SO(10), where anomaly cancellation is automatic from group theory).

question: Custodial $SU(2)$ symmetry: what is it and why does $\rho = 1$ at tree level?

options: Higgs sector has accidental global $SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$ symmetry; vev breaks to diagonal $SU(2)_{L+R}$ = custodial. This protects $\rho = M_W^2/(M_Z^2\cos^2\theta_W) = 1$ at tree level; broken by $U(1)_Y$ gauging and Yukawa asymmetry — gives small loop corrections $\Delta\rho \propto m_t^2 - m_b^2$
$\rho = 1$ is gauge-fixing artefact, not a physical statement (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Custodial is QCD's chiral symmetry; same idea (true only as a special case, not in general) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
$\rho = 1$ holds only if all Yukawas are equal (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: Write Higgs as $\Phi = (\phi^+, \phi^0)^T$, regroup into bidoublet $M = (\tilde\Phi, \Phi)$ with $\tilde\Phi = i\sigma_2\Phi^*$. Then $\text{tr}(M^\dagger M)$ has global $SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$: $M \to LMR^\dagger$. Vev $\langle M\rangle = v\mathbb 1/\sqrt 2$ breaks to diagonal $SU(2)_V$ — custodial. Custodial guarantees $M_W = M_Z\cos\theta_W$ even after EWSB. Explicitly broken by: (i) gauging only $U(1)_Y \subset SU(2)_R$, contributes $\Delta\rho \sim \sin^2\theta_W \alpha/\pi$; (ii) Yukawa $y_t \neq y_b$ → $\Delta\rho \sim 3G_F m_t^2/(8\pi^2\sqrt 2) \sim 0.01$ — measured, agrees with SM, contributed to top-mass prediction before discovery. Hint: BSM models must usually preserve custodial to satisfy $\rho \approx 1$.
